MINI MINUTES:

Attorney, Claire Cochran of Law Offices of Daniel Feder (415-391-9476) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Lee Szor of Fox Rothschild LLP (415-364-5571) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT SUTTER WEST BAY HOSPITALS d/b/a California Pacific Medical Center's MOTION FOR ATTORNEY FEES AND EXPENSES is DENIED. Defendant California Pacific Medical Center fails to show that attorneys' fees are warranted under Williams v. Chino Valley Independent Fire District (2015) 61 Cal.4th 97, 99-100. Plaintiff's action against CPMC was not objectively groundless. At one time in her career, plaintiff worked for CPMC and she continued to receive documents that bore CPMC's name throughout her career. (Feder Dec. Ex. 3.) I also DENY plaintiff's request for attorneys' fees. Prevailing party to prepare order in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard Ulmer; Clerk: Jose Rios Merida; Reporter: Laura Martinez, CSR #11332, Bus. Tel: 415-551-3745. (D302)

Atty. LEE B. SZOR, ESQ. of Fox Rothschild LLP, Tel No. 415-364-5540 appearing for Defendants California Pacific Medical Center, and Sutter Pacific Medical Foundation.

Matter was not argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. As to DEFENDANT SUTTER WEST BAY HOSPITALS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication, Defendant CPMC's motion for summary judgment is granted. Defendant satisfied its summary judgment burden by presenting admissible evidence that CPMC is an entity distinct from SPMF and SPMF was plaintiff's employer. Plaintiff fails to create a triable issue that CPMC was plaintiff's joint employer. The CCP 437c(h) request for a continuance request is denied. Plaintiff does not provide a declaration describing what facts it may be able to obtain to oppose the motion and that plaintiff has acted diligently. (Cooksey v. Alexakis (2004) 123 Cal.App.4th 246, 257). ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976 (302/HK)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Feb-19-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. CLAIRE COCHRAN, ESQ. of the Law Offices of Daniel Feder, Tel No. 415-391-9476 appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. LEE B. SZOR, ESQ. of Fox Rothschild LLP, Tel No. 415-364-5540 appearing for Defendants California Pacific Medical Center, and Sutter Pacific Medical Foundation.

Matter argued and submitted by counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. SPMF's motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternative motion for summary adjudication is granted as to issues 5, 6 and 7 and denied as to all other issues. As to issue 1, there is a triable issue whether the continuing violation exception to the statute of limitations applies to plaintiff's FEHA claims. As to issues 2, 3, 4, 8 and 9, there are triable issues precluding summary adjudication of plaintiff's disability discrimination and failure to accommodate causes of action. Plaintiff has presented admissible evidence that her disability and leave requests were a motivating factor in the termination of her employment and that SPMF did not adequately respond to her accommodation requests and at times, was hostile to those requests. This evidence includes the following: when plaintiff returned from leave in 2011, she was demoted to a patient service representative; when plaintiff
returned from leave in 2013, she was moved from her desk-which had ergonomic
accommodations-and told that she did not need any accommodations; on July 17, 2013 plaintiff presented a medical leave request to her supervisors that showed that she would be out until September 3, 2103; and when plaintiff returned to work, she was wrongly accused of abandoning her job. (Plaintiff's declaration paragraphs 5, 8-14, 19-20, 23, and 36-41). Discrimination can also be inferred based on the temporal proximity of plaintiff's leave in September 2013 and the termination of her employment which occurred shortly thereafter. As to issues 5, 6, and 7, SPMF has presented admissible evidence of a legitimate reason for the termination of
plaintiff's employment and plaintiff has not present evidence creating a triable issue
that the termination was based on plaintiff's age. As to issue 10, there is a triable
issue regarding the underlying alleged misconduct. As to issue 11, there is a triable issue whether plaintiff's protected activity (leave requests) was a motivating factor in the termination of her employment. As to issue 12, a trier of fact could infer harassment based on SPMF's hostility to plaintiff's leave requests, SPMF's retaliation after plaintiff's taking leave, and Ms. Hom's grabbing of plaintiff's shoulder. As to issues 13 and 14, FEHA does not displace common law remedies and there are triable issues whether plaintiff can establish a FEHA violation. As to issue 15, there is a triable issue whether the termination of plaintiff's employment was based on plaintiff's leave requests. As to issue 16, there is a triable issue whether defendant discriminated against plaintiff based on plaintiff's disabilities and whether that discrimination constitutes extreme and outrageous conduct. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976 (302/HK)
